passage: For centuries, cartographers relied on astronomical observations and painstaking triangulation surveys to produce accurate maps of coastlines and interior regions. The introduction of satellite geodesy in the mid-twentieth century changed everything [3]precise coordinates could now be determined within meters anywhere on Earth it became possible to compile global databases of topographic data that superseded all previous mapping efforts. Today, satellite-derived datasets underpin nearly every navigational system in use.
stem: Which choice best maintains sentence boundaries and punctuation conventions?

choices.label: B
choices.text: precise coordinates could now be determined within meters anywhere on Earth, it became possible

choices.label: C
choices.text: precise coordinates could now be determined within meters anywhere on Earth. It became possible

choices.label: D
choices.text: precise coordinates could now be determined within meters anywhere on Earth; and it became possible

explanation: The underlined portion contains two independent clauses that have been run together with no punctuation between them: 'precise coordinates could now be determined within meters anywhere on Earth' (subject: 'coordinates,' verb: 'could be determined') and 'it became possible to compile global databases of topographic data that superseded all previous mapping efforts' (subject: 'it,' verb: 'became'). Fusing two independent clauses without any punctuation or conjunction creates a run-on sentence, which is the error in Choice A. Choice B inserts only a comma between the two independent clauses, producing a comma splice — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. Choice C correctly places a period after the first independent clause and capitalizes the second, producing two grammatically complete sentences. Choice D uses 'semicolon + and,' which is non-standard: a semicolon already signals that two independent clauses are being connected, so adding a coordinating conjunction ('and') is redundant and incorrect in Standard English.

distractorRationale.A: The original presents two independent clauses fused together with no punctuation — a run-on sentence. 'Precise coordinates could now be determined within meters anywhere on Earth' and 'it became possible to compile global databases' are both independent clauses that require a proper boundary between them.
distractorRationale.B: Inserting a comma between the two independent clauses ('...anywhere on Earth, it became possible...') creates a comma splice. A comma alone is insufficient to join two independent clauses in Standard English.
distractorRationale.C: Correct. Placing a period after the first independent clause ('...anywhere on Earth.') and beginning a new sentence with 'It became possible...' correctly separates the two independent clauses and eliminates the run-on.
distractorRationale.D: Using 'semicolon + and' ('...anywhere on Earth; and it became possible...') is non-standard. A semicolon already functions as a connector between two independent clauses; pairing it with the coordinating conjunction 'and' is redundant and incorrect in Standard English conventions.
